coredata.dc:title: Remarkably boosting the lubricity of polyalphaolefin by loading amphiphilic carbon dots stabilized by Span-80
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Baogang
Zhang, Lilong
Dai, Shanshan
Lu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The amphiphilic carbon dots (A-CDs) derived from Tween-80 were used as the lubricant additives of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) with the aid of Span-80. The Span-80 (5.0 wt%) endowed A-CDs with super dispersibility in PAO4 by the hydrogen bond interactions between the hydrophilic head groups of Span-80 and the oxygen-containing groups on A-CDs. At the load of 50 N, the biggest friction and wear reductions for PAO4 triggered by the A-CDs (1.3 wt%) stabilized by Span-80 (5.0 wt%) were 45.4 and 69.8%, respectively. The distinguished tribological performance of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 illustrated the co-lubrication effect of Span-80 and A-CDs. The wear scar surface analysis results revealed that the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 distinctly alleviated the adhesive and abrasive wear of friction pair. The favorable smoothness and high O content on wear scar surface lubricated by A-CDs suspension reflected the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 have participated in the formation of effective lubricating films. The reasonable lubrication mechanism of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 was discussed based on the co-lubrication effect of Span-80 and A-CDs. The absorbing effect of Span-80 and the rolling and filling effects of A-CDs are all beneficial to the building of lubricating films, thereby tremendously boosting the lubricity of PAO4. The A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 are promising to become a kind of eco-friendly and high-performance lubricant additive for PAO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Tween-80
Span-80
Lubricant additive
Friction reduction
Wear resistance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological tests and worn surface analyses
Wear scar surface analyses and lubrication mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (c) survey XPS spectrum, fine XPS (d) C1s and (e) O1s spectra of A-CDs. (b) FTIR spectra and (f) TGA curves of A-CDs and Tween 80. Insets of (a): high-resolution TEM image and particle size distribution diagram of A-CDs.

Optical photos of (a) A-CDs suspension (1.3 wt%) in PAO4 and (b) A-CDs suspension (1.3 wt%) in PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80 after preparation (left) and standing for 2 weeks (right). (c) Schematic plot of dispersion state of A-CDs in PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80.

(a) COF curves of PAO4, PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80, A-CDs suspensions in PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 at the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min. (b) Mean COF and WV of A-CDs/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension varying with the c. Inset of (b): Mean COF and WV of PAO4, PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension.

Micrographs, 2D and 3D profiles of wear scars of the disks lubricated by (a) PAO4, (b) PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 and (c) A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension at the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min.
(a) Mean COF and (b) WV values of PAO4, PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension at the loads of 50–250 N and duration of 20 min.

SEM images with low (left) and high (right) magnifications of wear scars lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension under the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min.
Selected area EDS elemental mappings of wear scar surfaces lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension under the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min.
XPS (a) Fe2p, (b) C1s and (c) O1s spectra of wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension under the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min.
Schematic plot of synthesis process of A-CDs.
Schematic illustration of the lubrication mechanism of A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension.
EDS elemental compositions of wear scar surfaces lubricated by PAO4 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension under the load of 50 N and duration of 20 min.

head.ce:title: Remarkably boosting the lubricity of polyalphaolefin by loading amphiphilic carbon dots stabilized by Span-80

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amphiphilic carbon dots (A-CDs) derived from Tween-80 were used as the lubricant additives of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) with the aid of Span-80. The Span-80 (5.0 wt%) endowed A-CDs with super dispersibility in PAO4 by the hydrogen bond interactions between the hydrophilic head groups of Span-80 and the oxygen-containing groups on A-CDs. At the load of 50 N, the biggest friction and wear reductions for PAO4 triggered by the A-CDs (1.3 wt%) stabilized by Span-80 (5.0 wt%) were 45.4 and 69.8%, respectively. The distinguished tribological performance of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 illustrated the co-lubrication effect of Span-80 and A-CDs. The wear scar surface analysis results revealed that the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 distinctly alleviated the adhesive and abrasive wear of friction pair. The favorable smoothness and high O content on wear scar surface lubricated by A-CDs suspension reflected the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 have participated in the formation of effective lubricating films. The reasonable lubrication mechanism of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 was discussed based on the co-lubrication effect of Span-80 and A-CDs. The absorbing effect of Span-80 and the rolling and filling effects of A-CDs are all beneficial to the building of lubricating films, thereby tremendously boosting the lubricity of PAO4. The A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 are promising to become a kind of eco-friendly and high-performance lubricant additive for PAO.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction losses in engine and transmission systems consume more than one sixth of fuel energy for a car [1]. The formulated engine oil provided necessary lubrication for the critical parts of engine can not only save the energy by reducing friction but also prolong the service life of engine by alleviating wear. The polyalphaolefin (PAO) is a kind of high-grade base oil for blending engine oil because of its extremely low pour point, high oxidation stability and superb low-temperature fluidity. Although the PAO is particularly preeminent among various base oils such as mineral, animal, vegetable and ester oils, itself can hardly meet the lubrication demand under the boundary lubrication regime since the oil film between the friction interfaces is too thin to avoid the direct contact of the asperities [2]. Hence, the lubricant additives especially extreme pressure and anti-wear additives are indispensable ingredients for formulated engine oil [3–7]. The zinc dialkyldithiophosphates (ZDDPs) have been the most famous additives since 1930s. Despite ZDDPs are attracting more and more concerns including pollution, ash generation and catalyst poisoning, the lack of success in finding a replacement for ZDDPs continues until now due to their low cost and outstanding multi-functionality. Therefore, the development of eco-friendly and high-performance additive for PAO is still an urgent work.

The building of boundary lubricating films on rubbing surfaces is the only way to realize effective lubrication under the boundary lubrication conditions. To achieve this mission, the organic additives for PAO including ZDDPs, ionic liquids and organic molybdenum compounds need to react with the friction interfaces to create the tribofilms [8–16]. The active elements of P, S and halogen are usually inevitable in the molecules of organic additives, which will bring about the troublesome pollution and corrosion problems. The nanoadditives possess an innate advantage over organic additives, that is, they can construct the robust boundary lubrication films on rubbing surfaces by filling, depositing and sintering. Once the dispersibility problem of nanoadditives was solved by surface modification and/or adding dispersant, the metal, oxide and sulfide nanoparticles [17–27], 2D materials such as the BN, layered double-hydroxide, graphene, organic framework and black phosphorus [28–40], carbon dots (CDs) [41–44], and the composites have been used as the prominent additives of PAO [2,45–50]. Thereinto, the CDs are attracting more and more attention owing to their small and uniform particle size, abundant surface groups, favorable biocompatibility and outstanding film-building ability. By virtue of the abundant surface groups, the CDs were directly used as the super lubricant additives of H2O and polyethylene glycol without help of surface modification and dispersant [51–65]. However, the surface modification was always inevitable for application of CDs as additives of PAO because the PAO is completely non-polar [41–44]. So far, the synthesis and purification of oil-soluble CDs obtained by surface modification is still a big challenge, which greatly limits the application potential of CDs. Although adding dispersant is a more promising mean to disperse nanoadditives in PAO, it is still a support strategy due to its relatively unsatisfactory efficiency. Hence, the work on application of CDs as additives of PAO with the aid of dispersant has not appeared yet.

Recently, Jiang et al. have synthesized a kind of amphiphilic CDs (A-CDs) by using Tween-80 as the precursors with the convenient synthesis and purification processes [66]. Considering that there are abundant hydrophobic carbon chains and oxygen-containing groups on A-CDs, the A-CDs are apt to gain good dispersibility in PAO once the intimate interactions between oxygen-containing groups of A-CDs and hydrophilic head groups of amphiphilic dispersants, thereby becoming the eco-friendly and high-performance additive for PAO. Inspired by the opinion, herein, the A-CDs were synthesized according to the method of Jiang et al. with a few improvements (Scheme 1 ). Then the A-CDs were used as the additives of PAO4 with the aid of right amount of Span-80. The tribological performance of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 was investigated in detail by varying the loading concentration of A-CDs and the applied load. The lubrication mechanism of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 as additives of PAO4 was also proposed based on the experimental and wear scar surface analysis results.

Tween-80, concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), petroleum ether (boiling point: 60–90 °C), Span-80 and anhydrous ethanol were bought from Chengdu Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were analytical reagents and directly used as received. The synthetic base oil of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) was provided by the Mobil. The ultrapure water (about 18.25 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experimental process.

The A-CDs were facile synthesized according to the reported method with a few improvements [66]. Typically, Twee-80 (10 g), concentrated H2SO4 (10 mL) and H3PO4 (30 mL) were fully mixed in a round-bottom flask (150 mL) by magnetic stirring. Then, the flask was immersed into an oil-bath and kept at 90 °C for 4 h under the magnetic stirring. After cooling down to the room temperature, a large amount of ultrapure water was added into the flask. Vast brown oily matters were appeared at the bottom of flask. The crude oily products were sufficiently washed by ultrapure water (20 mL) for 6 times to remove the remaining acids and water-soluble by-products. To purify the crude products, 30 mL of petroleum ether was introduced to form a brown dispersion liquid with a lot of precipitates. The precipitates and solvent in the dispersion liquid were eliminated in sequence by the filtration and rotary evaporation. Finally, the A-CDs presented as a black brown gel was obtained with a relatively high yield (28.1%).

The morphologies, structures and compositions of A-CDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G20), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, WQF-520), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250), and thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, STA449F3). The viscosity of PAO4, PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension was measured on a Bohlin CVO rotational rheometer under the fixed shear rate (7.34 s−1), certain duration (20 s), parallel-plate mode and room temperature (10–15 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological tests and worn surface analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological performance of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 (5.0 wt%) as the additives of PAO4 were systematacially evaluated on a universal mechanical tester (UMT, Bruker) platform under the ball-on-disk linear reciprocating sliding module and steel/steel contact. The upper running ball (12.7 mm) and lower stationary disk (50 mm × 35 mm × 4 mm) were both made of AISI 52100 bearing steel with the hardness of about 59–61 HRC. The applied load was 50–250 N, which resulted in the initial maximum Hertzian contact pressure of 1.87–3.20 GPa. The mean sliding speed, test duration and amplitude were 10 mm/s, 20 min and 5 mm, respectively. All of friction tests were performed at room temperature and under atmospheric environment. Prior to frictional test, the ball and disk were ultrasonically cleaned in the anhydrous ethanol for 10 min. Then, the lubricant (0.1 mL) was dropped onto the ball-disk contact zone. During the frictional process, the friction coefficients were recorded automatically by a data-acquiring system. Each friction test was repeated for three times. The mean friction coefficient was calculated as the average value of all the acquired data points. After friction test, the lower disk was cleaned in anhydrous ethanol by ultrasonic treatment. The micrographs, 2D and 3D profiles of wear scar on disk were gained by a white light interferometer. The wear volume of disk was calculated as the product of 2D profile integral area and amplitude. The micro-topography and elemental composition of wear scar surface were analyzed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7500F) equipped with the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) and the XPS spectrometer.

Fig. 1a gives the TEM image of A-CDs. The A-CDs with morphology of nearly spherical particle have been successfully synthesized. According to the particle size distribution diagram (inset of Fig. 1a), the average diameter of A-CDs is about 14.6 nm. Compared with the former CDs gained by the similar method [66], the A-CDs possess a bigger diameter due to the longer reaction time. In addition, the yield of A-CDs was also much higher despite they have experienced an extraction process. The high-resolution TEM image of A-CDs (inset of Fig. 1a) reflects that the crystallinity of A-CDs is poor, which is a commonplace for the CDs synthesized by the “bottom up” method [67–69].

Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of Tween-80 and A-CDs. The feature peaks at 3498, 2928, 2858 and 1732 cm−1, assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH in hydroxyl group, CH in –CH2–, CH in –CH3 and CO in ester group, respectively, are found in the spectrum of Tween-80. Moreover, a series of strong peaks below 1400 cm−1 belonging to the deformation vibrations of CH and CO are appeared (marked out by black dotted ellipse). These results are well consistent with the molecular structure of Tween-80. In the spectrum of A-CDs, the peaks at 2928 and 2858 cm−1 are still existent, whereas almost no peak below 1400 cm−1 can be observed, hinting that the hydropholic carbon chain and hydrophilic oxygen-containing moieties of Tween-80 are survived and decomposed, respectively, during the synthesis process of A-CDs. In addition, the peaks at 1705 and 1652 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC. The irregular peak above 3500 cm−1 can be due to the existence of free hydroxyl groups and hydrogen bonds [51]. The peak at 1457 cm−1 can be resulted in by deformation vibrations of OH in hydroxyl group. These results indicate that the surface groups on A-CDs include not only hydropholic carbon chain but also hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups such as carbonyl and hydroxyl groups.

The survey XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 2c illustrates that the A-CDs are mainly composed of C and O elements. No S and P elements are detected, reflecting that the purification process of A-CDs is desired. The fine XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows that the mainly chemical states of C in A-CDs are C-C/C=C accompanied with low amounts of CO and CO. The O elements in the forms of OH, CO and CO species are emerged in the fine XPS O1s spectrum (Fig. 2e), which is well in line with the results of FTIR spectra.

The TGA curve of Tween-80 in Fig. 2f gives two stages of weight loss at about 207 and 345 °C, which can be attributed to the thermal decomposition of hydrophilic oxygen-containing moieties and hydropholic carbon chain, respectively. The first weight loss stage beginning at 207 °C for Tween-80 is disappeared in the TGA curve of A-CDs, further suggesting that the hydrophilic oxygen-containing moieties of Tween-80 precursors were almost decomposed during the synthesis process of A-CDs. The only weight loss stage for A-CDs beginning at 345 °C is owing to the thermal decomposition of surface groups of A-CDs. It is worth noting that the A-CDs are almost burned out at the temperature of 500 °C because of their poor crystallinity.

The as-synthesized A-CDs were considered to be hydropholic as they underwent a petroleum ether extraction process. However, the A-CDs are amphiphilic according to the characterization results of Fig. 1b. The amphiphilicity of A-CDs is visually confirmed by the photos shown in Fig. 2a. Although the A-CDs can be dispersed into PAO4 by ultrasonic treatment, the generated A-CDs suspension (1.3 wt%) is turbid and the A-CDs are completely settled out from PAO4 after standing for two weeks. To further improve the dispersibility of A-CDs in PAO4, the common surfactant of Span-80 with similar amphiphilicity is introduced. When the A-CDs are ultrasonically dispersed into PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80, a transparent suspension just like solution can be obtained (Fig. 2b). Moreover, this suspension keeps stable even it is standing for more than 3 months under the room temperature. The ultrahigh dispersibility of A-CDs in PAO4 containing Span-80 can be due to the intimate interactions between A-CDs and Span-80 molecules (Fig. 2c). The reasonable hydrogen bond interaction between the oxygen-containing group of A-CDs and hydrophilic head group of Span-80 molecule may play a significant role in improving the dispersibility of A-CDs in PAO4. Considering that the dispersibility is the most critical issue for nanoadditives [70–72], the A-CDs with the aid of Span-80 can be a kind of promising lubricant additive for PAO.

The preliminary experiment demonstrated that at least 5.0 wt% of Span-80 was needed to ensure the favorable dispersibility of A-CDs (2.0 wt%) in PAO4. Therefore, the tribological performance of A-CDs as additives of PAO4 was evaluated actually by using PAO4 with 5.0 wt% of Span-80 as base oil. The coefficient of friction (COF) curve of PAO4 in Fig. 3a displays an obvious running-in period and a rising trend with the increasing time. Moreover, this curve is high and fluctuated, reflecting the unfavorable lubricity of PAO4 under the boundary lubrication regime. The COF curve of PAO4 with 5.0 wt% of Span-80 is lower and smoother than that of PAO4, verifying the certain friction reduction effect of Span-80. Compared with that of PAO4 with 5.0 wt% of Span-80, the COF curves of A-CDs suspensions in PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 go down again when the loading concentrations (c) of A-CDs are higher than 0.5 wt%, confirming the obvious friction reduction function of A-CDs. What is more, the COF curves of A-CDs suspensions at c of 1.0 and 1.3 wt% even exhibit a declining trend after the running-in period, implying the gradual formation of effective lubricating films between the rubbing interfaces. The climbing trend appears again in the COF curves of A-CDs suspensions with c of 1.5 and 2.0 wt%, implying that the optimum c for A-CDs as additives is existent. Fig. 3b displays that both the mean COF and WV of A-CDs suspensions reduce first and then rise with the increasing c, determining the optimum c of 1.3 wt%. It is well known that the amounts of nanoadditives can greatly influence their lubricating performances. When the amount of A-CD is too low, there is no enough A-CD to form the effective lubricating films. However, the excess A-CDs will aggregate and result in abrasive wear. To clarify the friction reduction and anti-wear functions of the Span-80 and A-CDs, the mean COF and WV of PAO4, PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension are listed in the inset of Fig. 3b. 5 wt% of Span-80 makes the COF and WV of PAO4 reduce by 24.5 and 36.8%, respectively, whereas the COF and WV reductions of PAO4 jointly trigged by 5 wt% of Span-80 and 1.3 wt% of A-CDs are 45.4 and 69.8%, respectively, suggesting that the A-CDs and Span-80 play the significant co-lubrication effect as the additives of PAO4. As shown in Fig. S1, it is worth noting that the mean viscosity values of PAO4, PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension are 108.2, 111.4 and 115.2 mPa·s, respectively. Obviously, the viscosity variation of PAO4 caused by the additives is very limited (<7%), which can hardly result in a change of lubricating regime and hence cannot be the main reason of the remarkable lubricity enhancement of PAO4. Under the similar test conditions, the performance of A-CDs (1.3 wt%) stabilized by Span-80 (5 wt%) is far beyond the ionic liquid additive [73], much better than hexadecylamine modified CDs [44], and comparable to the Fe3O4/BN composite additive [74]. Specifically, in our previous work, the traditional additives of ZDDP (1 wt%) only made the COF and WV of PAO4 reduce by 30.7% and 19.9% [44], respectively, which is much inferior to the additives reported here.

Fig. 4 intuitively reflects the remarkable wear resistance abilities of A-CDs (1.3 wt%) as additives of PAO4. The 2D profile in Fig. 4a shows that the wear scar lubricated by PAO4 has the large width of 0.38 mm and depth of 4.58 μm accompanied by deep scratches, implying the occurrence of severe adhesive wear owing to the direct contact of friction interfaces. Compared with the wear scar shown in Fig. 4a, the depth of wear scar lubricated with PAO4 containing 5.0 wt% of Span-80 (Fig. 4b) is greatly reduced to 2.11 μm. Besides, the disappearance of deep scratches also embodies the favorable wear resistance ability of Span-80, which can be due to the physical absorption of Span-80 molecules on the rubbing surfaces. The width and depth of wear scar lubricated with A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension (Fig. 4c) are sharply reduced to 0.30 mm and 1.27 μm, respectively, confirming the distinguished anti-wear function of A-CDs. It is worth noting that the integrated area of 2D profile in Fig. 4c is less than one third of integrated area in Fig. 4a and one half of integrated area in Fig. 4b.

At the optimum c of 1.3 wt% and duration of 20 min, the effects of applied load on the tribological performance of A-CDs were studied. The specific COF curves of PAO4, PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension at various loads are shown in Fig. S2. The COF curves of PAO4 and PAO4 containing 5 wt% of Span-80 are declined with the increasing load. Whereas the COF curves of suspension are tangled with each other when the loads are 100–250 N. As shown in Fig. 5 , although both the mean COF and WV of A-CDs suspension are always lower than that of PAO4 and PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 under the various loads, the COF and WV differences between the suspension and PAO4 are shrunken with the increasing load from 50 to 200 N. The friction and wear reductions for PAO4 triggered by 5 wt% of Span-80 and 1.3 wt% of A-CDs are 45.4 and 69.8% at the load of 50 N, whereas these values reduce to 25.7 and 14.2% when the load is 200 N. Despite the wear reduction slightly increase to 23.3% at the load of 250 N, the A-CDs exhibit the best friction reduction and wear resistance performance at the mild load of 50 N. The weaken performance under higher loads is attributed to the relatively common mechanical strengths of the lubricating films formed by A-CDs and Span-80. The lubricating films are prone to be worn out under high loads and loss their friction-reducing and anti-wear functions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear scar surface analyses and lubrication mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To gain deep understanding of lubrication mechanism of A-CDs in PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80, the wear scar surfaces lubricated by PAO4 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%) suspension in PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 were analyzed by SEM, EDS and XPS technologies. Some wear debris and grooves can be found in the SEM images of wear scar lubricated by PAO4 (Fig. 6a), meaning the existence of severe abrasive and adhesive wear. In sharp contrast, the wear scar surface lubricated with A-CDs (1.3 wt%) suspension in PAO4 with 5 wt% of Span-80 is smooth with slight scratches (Fig. 6b), implying the presence of effective lubricating film on the wear scar.

The regions of SEM images for EDS analyses were marked out by the red rectangles in Fig. 6. The EDS elemental mappings shown in Fig. 7 demonstrate that both of the wear scar surfaces are covered by Fe, Cr, C and O elements. The uniform distributions of C and O elements on wear scar lubricated by A-CDs suspension further hint the existence of effective lubricating film. The specific EDS elemental compositions of wear scar surfaces are also listed in Table 1 . For wear scar lubricated by PAO4, the Fe and low amount of Cr elements are derived from the steel substrate. A small amount of O element comes from the O2 in air. The large amount of C element means that the decomposition of PAO4 must happen during the friction process. Although there is abundant amorphous carbon on wear scar lubricated with PAO4, the effective lubricating film can hardly form because of the poor mechanical property of amorphous carbon. The content of O element on the wear scar lubricated by A-CDs suspension is distinctly higher than that on the wear scar lubricated with PAO4, which can be attributed to absorption/decomposition of Span-80 and deposition of A-CDs.

The chemical states of main elements on wear scar surfaces lubricated by PAO4 and A-CDs (1.3 wt%)/PAO4 (5 wt% of Span-80) suspension were uncovered by the XPS elemental spectra. Fig. 8a shows that the Fe element on both of the wear scars mainly exists in the forms of FeO and Fe2O3, reflecting the oxidation of steel surface. Low amounts of FeC and metallic Fe species can also be detected out. The C species of CO and C-C/C=C on the wear scar lubricated by PAO4 (Fig. 8b) can be attributed to the decomposition of PAO4. The mildly higher contents of CO and CO species on the wear scar lubricated by suspension (Fig. 8b) are derived from the Span-80 and A-CDs. The same O species of CO and Fe-O/Cr-O are found on both of the wear scars (Fig. 8c). Generally, the XPS analysis results on wear scars lubricated by PAO4 and suspension are similar since the tribochemical products on both of the wear scars have the same elemental composition of Fe, C and O.

On account of the above results of frictional tests and wear scar surface analyses, the lubrication mechanism of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 as additives of PAO4 is discussed (Scheme 2 ). By virtue of their excellent dispersibility in PAO4, the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 play the significant rolling effect between the asperities of rubbing surfaces, remarkably alleviating the adhesive wear of friction pair. In addition, once the A-CDs/Span-80 structures are broken down by the violent shear force, the Span-80 molecules can spontaneously absorb onto the rubbing surfaces via their polar head groups as the metal friction interfaces are positively charged due to the effusion of low-energy electrons. At the same time, the A-CDs can enter and deposit into the gaps between asperities of rubbing surfaces and play the filling effect [57,62,75]. These absorbing and filling effects are continuous owing to the uninterrupted supply of A-CDs/Span-80 structures. The complex tribochemical reactions between the friction pair and lubricant will occur because of the high contact pressure and vast frictional heat. The absorbing, rolling and filling effects of Span-80 and A-CDs are all beneficial to the building of tribofilms on the rubbing interfaces, which tremendously reduce the friction and wear of friction pair. In other words, as a result of the co-lubrication effect of Span-80 and A-CDs, the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 as the additives significantly boost the lubricity of PAO4.

The A-CDs were successfully synthesized with the relatively high yield and applied as additives of PAO4 with the aid of Span-80. At the load of 50 N, the A-CDs (1.3 wt%) stabilized by Span-80 (5.0 wt%) made the COF and WV of PAO4 reduce by 45.4 and 69.8%, respectively, demonstrating the distinguished tribological performance. Although the friction and wear reduction functions of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 were gradually attenuated with the increasing load, the improvement for the lubricity of PAO4 triggered by the co-lubrication effect of A-CDs and Span-80 was still obvious. The wear scar surface analyses revealed that the A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 in PAO4 greatly alleviated the adhesive and abrasive wear, and hence resulting in the smooth wear scar surface. The abundant O content on wear scar surface lubricated by A-CDs suspension derived from the EDS analyses implied that the A-CDs and Span-80 should have participated in the formation of effective lubricating films on the rubbing surfaces. The lubrication mechanism of A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 as additives of PAO4 was proposed based on the co-lubrication effect of A-CDs and Span-80. The rolling and filling effects of A-CDs and absorbing effect of Span-80 molecules were all beneficial to the generation of effective lubricating films on the friction interfaces, and hence tremendously boosting the lubricity of PAO4. The A-CDs stabilized by Span-80 reported here are hopeful to become a kind of eco-friendly and high-performance lubricant additive for PAO.

Baogang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing-review & editing. Lilong Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-Original Draft. Shanshan Dai: Visualization, Writing-review & editing. Hongsheng Lu: Visualization, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.108924.
